title: Problems
summary: This page contains end-of-chapter problems from Chapter 1 covering signal properties including power calculations, even and odd periodic signals, complex-valued signals, and periodic signal construction. Problems involve sketching signals, determining fundamental periods, computing energy and power, and analyzing transformations of signals such as time scaling and shifting.

figures.caption: Two signals x1(t) and x2(t): (a) a piecewise-linear triangular signal peaking at 4 between t = -1 and t = 2, and (b) a signal defined by a t² curve from 0 to 2 reaching a peak of 4, followed by a linear ramp decreasing to -4 between t = 2 and t = 4.
figures.description: The figure has two time-domain subplots with horizontal axis t and vertical signal axes labeled x1(t) and x2(t). In (a), x1(t) is a triangular piecewise-linear signal: it is 0 at t = -1, rises linearly to 4 at t = 0, then falls linearly back to 0 at t = 2. In (b), x2(t) follows the curve t^2 from t = 0 to t = 2, reaching 4, then jumps to -4 at t = 2 and increases linearly to 0 at t = 4. Students should notice the different segment types (linear versus quadratic), the peak value 4, and the discontinuity in x2(t) at t = 2.
